coredata.dc:title: Label-free detection of ampicillin in milk and water using fluorescence carbon dots synthesised from Citrus Limetta peel
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Karthikeyan, Masilamani
Hemalatha, Muralikrishnan
Venkatasubbu, Devanand G.
Abimanyu, Sharma
Aravind, Menon
Rathinsabapathi, Pasupathi
coredata.dc:description: The detection of antibiotics is crucial for ensuring food safety and conducting environmental monitoring. However, the excessive presence of antibiotics like ampicillin can have harmful effects on both humans and ecosystems. This study introduces a new method for creating nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using Citrus limetta peels through a simplified hydrothermal process. The resulting N-CQDs emit a greenish blue fluorescent color, with excitation and emission wavelengths of 345 nm and 460 nm, respectively. The presence of ampicillin leads to fluorescence quenching in the N-CQDs due to the inner filter effect resulting from overlaps between the absorbance of ampicillin and the emission of N-CQDs. Using the developed biosensor, we have successfully detected ampicillin with an LOD of 2.2 μg/L. Furthermore, we confirmed the biosensor's effectiveness by detecting ampicillin in both milk and water samples. This environmentally friendly and cost-effective N-CQDs biosensor presents a promising approach for monitoring antibiotics in various food and environmental samples, especially demonstrating great potential for ampicillin detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ampicillin
Biosensor
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching
Food monitoring

Characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Sensitivity and selectivity analysis for ampicillin detection
Validation of biosensors in the detection of ampicillin
Synthesis and characterization of the synthesized N-CQDs
Optical characterization of N-CQDs and target detection
Sensitivity and selectivity assay for ampicillin
Validation of biosensor for detection of ampicillin in tap water and milk samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the preparation of N-CQDs.
(A) TEM images of N-CQDs, (B) Size distribution of N-CQDS, (C) UV–Vis spectrum of synthesized N-CQDs, (D) XRD Pattern, and (E) FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs.
Inner filter effect of N-CQDs for detecting ampicillin schematic representation.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of NCQD conjugated with Ampicillin. Insert NCQDs without and with UV irradiation, and (B) linear plot between ampicillin concentration and fluorescent intensity.
Selectivity analysis of NCQD with other antibiotics.
Comparison of ampicillin detection methods using nanosensors.
Detection of ampicillin in the spiked tap water and milk samples.

head.ce:title: Label-free detection of ampicillin in milk and water using fluorescence carbon dots synthesised from Citrus Limetta peel

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, 603 203 Chengalpattu Dist, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur – 603 203, Chengalpattu Dist, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of antibiotics is crucial for ensuring food safety and conducting environmental monitoring. However, the excessive presence of antibiotics like ampicillin can have harmful effects on both humans and ecosystems. This study introduces a new method for creating nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using Citrus limetta peels through a simplified hydrothermal process. The resulting N-CQDs emit a greenish blue fluorescent color, with excitation and emission wavelengths of 345 nm and 460 nm, respectively. The presence of ampicillin leads to fluorescence quenching in the N-CQDs due to the inner filter effect resulting from overlaps between the absorbance of ampicillin and the emission of N-CQDs. Using the developed biosensor, we have successfully detected ampicillin with an LOD of 2.2 μg/L. Furthermore, we confirmed the biosensor's effectiveness by detecting ampicillin in both milk and water samples. This environmentally friendly and cost-effective N-CQDs biosensor presents a promising approach for monitoring antibiotics in various food and environmental samples, especially demonstrating great potential for ampicillin detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been posing as major pollutants due to excessive consumption of antibiotics by humans and cattle, which are then excreted unchanged through faeces and urine into domestic sewage, contaminating the aquatic environment. β-Lactam antibiotics such as ampicillin have been generally used to treat bacterial infections in both cattle and humans due to their resilient antibacterial potency and low cost [1]. The consumption of antibiotic-contaminated diets severely affects humans. Earlier studies indicated that antibiotic levels exceeded the maximum allowable limit in food samples in many countries [2]. In a randomized clinical study investigating the adverse effects of the continual intake of ampicillin in different dosages injected into 400 children for seven days, moderate or mild diarrhea was common in patients, regardless of the dosage prescribed [3]. Erythematous maculopapular skin eruptions occurred in almost 3–7 % of patients, and urticarial skin allergies happened in 1 % of the patients [4].

The presence of ampicillin in water and edible products, such as milk, can be traced using various detection methods. Liquid chromatography paired with mass spectrometry or UV detections was used as a confirmatory method for ampicillin detection; however, they are expensive, time-consuming, and complicated [5]. Also, these techniques are unable to be used for large-scale screening. On the contrary, rapid detection techniques like ELISA, immunosensors, and colorimetric nanosensors were used to detect ampicillin in a large sample size. Recently, Silver nanoparticle (AgNP) based colorimetric biosensor was developed to detect ampicillin in milk and water samples [6]. However, the fluorescence biosensor had numerous advantages, such as higher sensitivity, lower signal-to-noise ratio, multiplexing ability, and wider detection range than the AgNP-based colorimetric biosensor [7,8]. Fluorescence signals can be amplified using techniques such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which allows for detecting low analyte concentrations. This increased sensitivity is particularly advantageous when working with samples with low analyte levels. Furthermore, fluorescent biosensors offer a broader dynamic range for quantification, allowing for precise measurements across a wide concentration range.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are used as reporting molecules in fluorescent biosensors [9,10]. CQDs have numerous advantages, including excellent chemical inertness, outstanding chemo- and photo-stability, promising biocompatibility, better water solubility, and less toxicity [11]. They also have unique fluorescent characteristics and less environmental impact, making them appropriate candidates for fluorescence biosensors [12]. Gedanken et al. synthesized fluorescent CQDs through the hydrothermal synthesis of ammonia and glucose. CQDs have been used in biosensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis of fluorescent ink, and drug delivery [13]. Green one-step hydrothermal techniques for producing CQDs have recently gained popularity due to their simplicity, low cost, time savings, non-pollution, and biocompatibility [14]. The hydrothermal approach is employed for synthesizing CQDs using papaya and watermelon juice as carbon precursors [15]. These methods are green, straightforward, quick, and ecologically beneficial [16]. CQDs were biosensors for sensing numerous antibiotics, biomolecules, and metal ions [17]. Heteroatomic doping, such as N-CQDs, enhances the fluorescence intensity and increases the surface area and affinity [18].

In this study, we used the Citrus limetta (mosambi) peels to synthesize the carbon quantum dots. The fruits are widely cultivated, and their peels are readily available as agricultural residues. Utilizing these peels for carbon dot synthesis reduces the reliance on non-renewable resources and supports sustainable production practices at a lower cost. The green synthesis of carbon dots from Citrus limetta peels offers multiple advantages, including waste utilization, renewable sourcing, non-toxicity, energy efficiency, versatility, and biodegradability. The synthesized carbon quantum dots were used to detect ampicillin detection in the food samples.

Fresh Citrus limetta was obtained from a local market, while Ethylenediamine, Glycine, Ampicillin, Rifampicin, Gentamicin, Vancomycin, Kanamycin, and Naringenin were purchased from Himedia India Pvt. Limited India. Sodium hydroxide and ethanol were sourced from SRL Pvt. Limited India. All chemicals used were of analytical grade and did not require further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Citrus limetta peels were washed with tap water and dried in the shade. The dried peels were then ground into a powder. A mixture of 10 g of Citrus limetta peel powder and 1 g of glycine was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was heated in a furnace at 200 °C for 12 h. After reaching room temperature, the resulting dark brown solution was micro-filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove any significant residues and stored in a dark container until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) using JEM-2100 plus, JEOL Japan, and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) using Zeta sizer, Japan techniques were employed to observe the size and surface morphology of the N-CQDs. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy using Shimadzu IR Tracer-100, Japan was used to analyze the functional groups present in the N-CQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a K-Alpha instrument with X-rays at 140 watts (Physical Electronics, USA) to evaluate the chemical compositions of synthesized NCQDs. The surface charges of the N-CQDs were determined using zeta potential measurements. The absorption spectrum after doping was analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer, Shimadzu UV-3600 plus, Japan. The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer using Shimadzu spectrophotometer, Japan. The quantum yield of the N-CQDs was calculated using quinine sulphate (QS) as a reference, employing a relative technique [19]. The absorbance values for both N-CQDs and QS were below 0.05. The fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm for all samples. The quantum yield of N-CQDs was calculated using the formula [15]:
(1)
Q x
=
Q st
A st
/
A x
I x
/
I st
η
2 x
/
η
2 st
where Q represents quantum yield, A denotes absorbance, I indicates fluorescence intensity, Qst represents the quantum yield of quinine sulphate (54 %), Ast and Ax are the absorbance values for QS and N-CQDs, respectively, and the ratio between η2st and η2x is equal to one.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity analysis for ampicillin detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensitivity of the biosensor, different concentrations of ampicillin solutions ranging from 1 to 10 ng/mL were prepared using phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.4). The N-CQD and ampicillin solutions were mixed in a 3:1 ratio and stirred continuously at 4 °C for 30 min. The absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CQDs detecting the various concentrations of ampicillin were analyzed. To assess the selectivity of the synthesized N-CQDs, various antibiotics, including rifampicin, vancomycin, kanamycin, and naringenin, were used at the same concentration (5 ng/mL) along with ampicillin. A negative control (blank) without any antibiotics was also included.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of biosensors in the detection of ampicillin
ce:section.ce:para:
Validation of the biosensor involved spiking ampicillin into tap water and milk samples. Tap water samples were collected and subjected to centrifugation at a maximum of 12,000 rpm for 30 min, followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove macro molecules and other environmental contaminants. The resulting water was used for the study. For milk samples, a mixture of 1 mL chloroform and 1 mL 10 % trichloroacetic acid was added to 2 mL of the milk sample to precipitate proteins and dissolve organic materials. The mixture was vortexed for 2 min, sonicated in an ice bath for 15 min, and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm at room temperature for 10 min. The supernatant solutions were stored for future use. The samples (n = 5) were incubated with N-CQDs and varying concentrations of ampicillin, and fluorescence emission spectra were measured at 460 nm. The recovery of spiked ampicillin concentration (Rc) using the biosensor was calculated using the following formula:
(2)
R
c .
=
C measured
/
C spiked
×
100
where Cmeasured –ampicillin concentration measured by biosensor and Cspiked – spiked ampicillin concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the synthesized N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs were synthesized using glycine and Citrus limetta fruit peel as nitrogen and carbon sources through a one-step hydrothermal process (Fig. 1 ). N-CQDs were characterised by their size, charge, surface group, and spectral and fluorescence properties. The target ampicillin was detected through inner filter effects arising from spectral overlaps between the absorbance of ampicillin and the emission spectra of N-CQD.

Characterizing the N-CQDs is crucial for understanding their structural and surface properties. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging reveals that the N-CQDs are spherical (Fig. 2A). The NCQDs disperse well and have a diameter ranging from 0.5 to 4.0 nm, with an average diameter of 2.7 nm (Fig. 2B). When exposed to 345 nm UV light, the N-CQDs emit intense greenish blue (Cyan) fluorescence that is visible to the naked eye. However, there is no detectable photoluminescence without UV irradiation, and the solution appears pale yellow. The fluorescence of N-CQDs enables its potential applications in optical sensing or imaging. Zeta potential measurement shows a slightly positive net surface charge of 0.3 mV for the N-CQDs, which can influence their interactions with other materials in biological or environmental systems. This observation suggests that the size of the N-CQDs is a critical factor in their greenish blue fluorescent emission [19]. The UV–visible spectrum indicates that the N-CQDs exhibit maximum absorption at a wavelength of 345 nm, with a small peak at 280 nm (Fig. 2C). The peak at 280 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition attributed to the CC bonds involving sp2 hybridized clusters. The n-π* transition of the CO and NH groups shows an absorbance peak at 345 nm [20].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the N-CQDs (Fig. 2D) reveals a prominent diffraction peak centered at approximately 22.7°, corresponding to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials. The interlayer distance (d-spacing) of the N-CQDs is around 0.41 nm, slightly larger than the 0.37 nm of bulk graphite. These findings provide evidence of abundant functional groups on the surface of the N-CQDs [21]. Consequently, the synthesized N-CQDs exhibit a moderately crystalline/irregular graphitic structure. Moreover, a diffraction peak near 42.5° indicates the presence of the carbon material's (100) lattice spacing in the N-CQDs, suggesting the incorporation of oxygen and nitrogen moieties on their surface [22]. XPS analyzed the chemical composition of synthesized NCQDs was shown in (Supplementary figure – Fig. S1). The spectrum reveals the presence of carbon (C1s) with binding energy at 289.28 eV, nitrogen (N1s) at 400.48 eV, and oxygen (O1s) at 530.48 eV. These peaks confirm the elemental composition and provide insights into the chemical environment of the developed NCQDs.

The surface functional groups of the N-CQDs were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2E). The first stretch in the frequency range of 4000–3344 cm−1 corresponds to NH and OH stretching, indicating the presence of primary and secondary amine functional groups [15]. The second stretch at 1670–1600 cm−1 represents the CC stretching [23]. The third stretch corresponds to NO stretching, suggesting the presence of functional groups such as oxygen and nitrogen on the surface of the N-CQD, nitroalkanes exhibit a frequency range of 1550–1365 cm−1, with 1550 cm−1 being a significant vibration frequency range [24]. The fourth stretch corresponds to CH ending with halo compounds within the 1000–650 cm−1 frequency range [25]. The fifth stretch represents CI and SH stretching, indicating the presence of halo compounds as functional groups. Furthermore, the stretches at 1550, 1550, and 1564 cm−1 indicate the vibration band of nitrobenzene in the case of N-CQDs. The peak at 1564 cm−1 suggests a strong interaction between the nitro group and the aromatic ring, indicating the presence of the nitrobenzene group. The characteristic stretch at 1550 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of nitroalkanes.

The FTIR spectra indicate that N-CQDs have important functional groups on their surfaces. After being doped, there is an increase in amine groups, which is shown by an additional peak in the FTIR spectra. These surface functional groups suggest that the N-CQDs have better biocompatibility and are highly soluble in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of N-CQDs and target detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy was employed to investigate the optical properties of N-CQDs. The excitation wavelength for N-CQDs was determined to be 345 nm, while the maximum emission wavelength was found to be 460 nm. The emission spectra of NCQDs at varying excitation wavelengths are given in (Supplementary Figure, Fig. S2). The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of ampicillin were also obtained. It was observed that as the concentration of ampicillin increased, there was a decrease in the emission intensity at 460 nm. This decrease in fluorescence intensity was attributed to the inner filter effect, which occurs due to the spectral overlaps between the absorbance of ampicillin and the emission spectra of N-CQDs at 460 nm (Fig. 3 ). The principle behind N-CQDs-based biosensing is the inner filter effect (IFE) which occurs when the quencher absorbs excited or emitted light due to their absorption bands overlapping with the fluorophores emission or the excitation bands. This technique is practical because the amount of fluorescence quenched can be controlled by adjusting the quencher concentration. It is worth noting that the fluorophore and quencher in an IFE system do not have to be covalently linked, making it more straightforward and adaptable than other systems like fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [26], where the donor and acceptor need to have a complementary geometry in addition to the overlap between the acceptor's absorption bands and the fluorophore's excitation or emission spectrum and the system has to be positioned at a specific distance [27].

The N-CQDs have a quantum yield of 54.6 %, which is significantly higher than other green emissive CQDs [28]. The optical characterization results indicated that N-CQDs possessed favourable fluorescence characteristics, including fluorescent wavelength, fluorescent lifespan, quantum yields, and stability, comparable to CQDs derived from other dietary sources [29]. It is worth noting that N-CQDs synthesized from Citrus limetta, a by-product of fruit processing, offered the advantages of being environmentally friendly and cost-effective. Previous studies have also reported that CQDs prepared from food ingredients are non-toxic and hold the potential for sensing applications [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity assay for ampicillin
ce:section.ce:para: This study has developed a biosensor that can detect ampicillin in on-field samples within just 10 min, which is much faster than other detection methods. The biosensor has high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting ampicillin. These findings suggest that this biosensor could be a promising alternative to more complex and less environmentally friendly instruments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of biosensor for detection of ampicillin in tap water and milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: The biosensor was tested using ampicillin-spiked water and milk samples to assess its performance. The results in Table 2 demonstrate the biosensor's ability to effectively detect ampicillin in both water and milk samples. The recovery percentages for ampicillin in tap water samples ranged from 80 to 95 % for concentrations ranging from 0.08 μg/mL to 3.4 μg/mL, with RSD values ranging from 1.89 % to 5.38 %. Similarly, the average recovery for ampicillin in milk samples, spanning concentrations of 0.09 to 3.46 μg/mL, fell between 90 % and 99 %, with RSD values ranging from 1.75 % to 4.57 %. These results establish the biosensor's capability to accurately and precisely detect ampicillin in water and milk samples.

Overall, using N-CQDs as a biosensor for detecting ampicillin offers numerous advantages over traditional detection methods. The biosensor is sensitive, selective, and provides rapid detection. It is also portable, allowing for on-site detection in the field, which reduces the need for expensive laboratory equipment and infrastructure. Furthermore, the biosensor can detect ampicillin in various samples, such as milk and water, with a detection limit of 2.2 ng/mL (2.2 μg/L). This makes it a valuable tool for monitoring antibiotic residues in food and environmental samples. The findings of this study open the door for further research into utilizing N-CQDs as biosensors for detecting other antibiotics and chemical contaminants. Further studies may be necessary to assess the biosensor's performance in detecting ampicillin in complex matrices, such as meat, fish, and other food products.

Masilamani Karthikeyan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Muralikrishnan Hemalatha: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Devanand G. Venkatasubbu: Resources. Sharma Abimanyu: Investigation. Menon Aravind: Investigation. Pasupathi Rathinsabapathi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
Ethical approval: This article contains no studies with human participants and animals performed by authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Chennai – 603 203, for their financial support for the project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111892.
